query: Merck clesrovimab FDA approval date infant RSV monoclonal antibody 2025

{"query":"Merck clesrovimab FDA approval date infant RSV monoclonal antibody 2025","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1754,"verdict":null,"extra_info":{"type":"featured_snippet","text":"On June 9, 2025, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved clesrovimab (Enflonsia, Merck), a second long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody, for prevention of RSV-associated LRTI among infants (11). Clesrovimab is administered as a single intramuscular injection before or during an infant's first RSV season.Aug 28, 2025"},"results":[{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/74/wr/mm7432a3.htm#:~:text=On%20June%209%2C%202025%2C%20the,an%20infant's%20first%20RSV%20season.","title":"Use of Clesrovimab for Prevention of Severe Respiratory Syncytial Virus–Associated Lower Respiratory Tract Infections in Infants: Recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices — ","content":"Nirsevimab (Beyfortus, Sanofi, in collaboration with AstraZeneca), a long-acting* monoclonal antibody for prevention of RSV-associated lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI), was recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) in August 2023; administration of RSV vaccine (Abrysvo, Pfizer) to pregnant women at 32–36 weeks’ gestation was recommended by ACIP in September 2023 (8).
During the 2024–2025 RSV season, the first full season when the two RSV prevention products were widely available, data from two national surveillance networks (the Respiratory Syncytial Virus Hospitalization Surveillance Network and the New Vaccine Surveillance Network) estimated that RSV-associated hospitalization rates among infants aged 0–7 months were 43% and 28% lower, respectively, compared with rates during pre–COVID-19 pandemic RSV seasons (10).
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved clesrovimab(Enflonsia, Merck), a second long-acting RSV monoclonal antibody, for prevention of RSV-associated LRTI among infants (11).
Clesrovimab is administered as a single intramuscular injection before or during an infant’s first RSV season.
Efficacy against RSV-associated LRTI hospitalization§§ was 90.9% (95% CI = 76.2%–96.5%), with the outcome occurring in five participants (0.2%) in the clesrovimab group and 27 (2.2%) participants in the placebo group (17,18).
URL addresses listed in MMWR were current as of the date of publication."},{"url":"https://www.merck.com/news/u-s-fda-approves-mercks-enflonsia-clesrovimab-cfor-for-prevention-of-respiratory-syncytial-virus-rsv-lower-respiratory-tract-disease-in-infants-born-during-or-entering-their-fir/","title":"U.S. FDA Approves Merck’s ENFLONSIA™ (clesrovimab-cfor) for Prevention of Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Lower Respiratory Tract Disease in Infants Born During or Entering Their First RSV Season - ","content":"RAHWAY, N.J.--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- Merck (NYSE: MRK), known as MSD outside of the United States and Canada, today announced the U.S.
“ENFLONSIA combines dosing convenience with strong clinical data showing significant reductions in RSV disease incidence and hospitalizations, making it a promising new intervention to help protect infants from RSV.”
ENFLONSIA should not be administered to infants with a history of serious hypersensitivity reactions, including anaphylaxis, to any component of ENFLONSIA.
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s (CDC’s) Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices is expected to meet later this month to discuss and make recommendations for the use of ENFLONSIA in infants.
Ordering is anticipated to begin in July, with shipments delivered before the start of the 2025-2026 RSV season."},{"url":"https://www.contagionlive.com/view/merck-s-clesrovimab-receives-fda-approval-to-protect-infants-from-rsv-during-first-season","title":"Merck’s Clesrovimab (Enflonsia) Receives FDA Approval to Protect Infants From RSV During First Season | Contagion Live","content":"News|Articles|June 11, 2025 (Updated: June 11, 2025)
Enflonsia, a single-dose, long-acting antibody, has been FDA approved to significantly reduce RSV infections and hospitalizations in infants, providing new hope for protecting vulnerable babies during RSV season.
Merck plans to make clesrovimab available for the 2025-26 RSV season, expanding preventative options beyond currently approved therapies.1
Enflonsia’s approval positions Merck alongside Sanofi and AstraZeneca’s Beyfortus, the first FDA-approved long-acting RSV antibody, which has shown 90% effectiveness against RSV hospitalizations."},{"url":"https://vaccines.phila.gov/index.php/enflonsia-clesrovimab-now-available-through-vfc/","title":"Enflonsia (Clesrovimab) Now Available Through VFC - Philadelphia Immunization Program","content":"On June 9, 2025, the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) licensed Merck’s Enflonsia (clesrovimab) for the prevention of respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) lower respiratory tract infection (LRTI)."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Merck clesrovimab FDA approval date infant RSV monoclonal antibody 2025","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.contemporarypediatrics.com/view/fda-approves-clesrovimab-to-protect-infants-during-first-rsv-season","title":"FDA approves clesrovimab to protect infants during first RSV season | Contemporary Pediatrics","content":"# FDA approves clesrovimab to protect infants during first RSV season. On June 9, 2025, the FDA approved Merck's monoclonal antibody clesrovimab (Enflonsia) to protect infants from respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) disease during their first RSV season, according to a press release from Merck.1,2. The biologics license application, accepted by the FDA on December 17, 2024, and subsequent approval from the federal agency were based on results from the phase 2b/3 CLEVER trial (MK-1654-004; NCT04767373), a randomized placebo-controlled trial evaluating a single dose of clesrovimab administered to healthy preterm and full-term infants. In addition, data from the phase 3 SMART trial (MK-1654-007) evaluating the safety and efficacy of clesrovimab vs palivizumab in infants and children at an increased risk for severe RSV disease was used in the application submission.2. 1. US FDA Approves Merck’s ENFLONSIA™ (clesrovimab-cfor) for Prevention of Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Lower Respiratory Tract Disease in Infants Born During or Entering Their First RSV Season.","score":0.94608605,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.guidelinecentral.com/insights/june-2025-enflonsia-clesrovimabcfor-med-alert","title":"Merck's Enflonsia (clesrovimab-cfor) Now Approved by FDA for RSV in ...","content":"Merck's Enflonsia, a new RSV preventive option for infants born during/before RSV season, was just approved by the FDA on June 9, 2025.","score":0.9055316,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/nda/2025/761432Orig1s000IntegratedR.pdf","title":"761432Orig1s000 - accessdata.fda.gov","content":"CENTER FOR DRUG EVALUATION AND RESEARCH APPLICATION NUMBER: 761432Orig1s000 INTEGRATED REVIEW BLA 761432 Enflonsia (clesrovimab-cfor) i Integrated Review Template, version 4.0 (11/1/23) Integrated Review Table 1. Application Information Application type BLA Application number(s) 761432 Priority or standard Priority Submit date(s) 10/10/2024 Received date(s) 10/10/2024 PDUFA goal date 6/10/2025 Division/office Division of Antivirals (DAV) Review completion date 6/5/2025 Established/proper name Clesrovimab-cfor (Proposed) proprietary name ENFLONSIA Pharmacologic class Monoclonal antibody Other product name(s) MK-1654 Applicant Merck Sharp & Dohme LLC Dosage form(s)/formulation(s) Solution for intramuscular (IM) injection Dosing regimen Single intramuscular dose of 105 mg Applicant-proposed indication(s)/ population(s) Prevention of RSV lower respiratory tract disease in neonates and infants born during or entering their first RSV season. SNOMED CT code for proposed indication disease term(s)1 55735094 Respiratory Syncytial Virus Infection (disorder) Regulatory action Approval Approved dosage (if applicable) 105 mg administered as a single intramuscular (IM) injection Approved indication(s)/ population(s) (if applicable) Prevention of RSV lower respiratory tract disease in neonates and infants born during or entering their first RSV season.","score":0.8963332,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.drugs.com/history/enflonsia.html","title":"Enflonsia (clesrovimab-cfor) FDA Approval History - Drugs.com","content":"**Enflonsia** (clesrovimab-cfor) is a respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) F protein-directed fusion inhibitor indicated for passive immunization for the prevention of RSV lower respiratory tract disease in neonates and infants who are born during or entering their first RSV season. * Enflonsia is a preventive, long-acting monoclonal antibody (mAb) designed to be administered at the same single dose (regardless of weight) to infants for direct, rapid and durable protection against a spectrum of RSV disease severity, including worsening disease requiring hospitalization. Approval was also supported by results from the Phase 3 SMART trial (MK-1654-007) evaluating the safety and efficacy of Enflonsia versus palivizumab in infants at increased risk for severe RSV disease. - For infants born outside of the RSV season, Enflonsia should be administered prior to the start of their first RSV season. | Jun  9, 2025 | Approval FDA Approves Enflonsia (clesrovimab-cfor) for Prevention of Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) Lower Respiratory Tract Disease in Infants |.","score":0.89302075,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.biopharmadive.com/news/merck-enflonsia-rsv-antibody-fda-approval-clesrovimab/750123","title":"Merck antibody drug for RSV approved by FDA","content":"Opens in a new window Opens an external website Opens an external website in a new window. Let BioPharma Dive’s free newsletter keep you informed, straight from your inbox. - [x]  By signing up to receive our newsletter, you agree to our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. # Merck antibody drug for RSV approved by FDA. Known as Enflonsia, the drug will compete for market share with Sanofi and AstraZeneca’s Beyfortus, which quickly became a blockbuster medicine following its approval two years ago. The Food and Drug Administration approved Merck & Co.’s RSV drug Enflonsia on June 9, 2025. Called Enflonsia and developed by Merck & Co., the drug is an antibody intended to prevent illness from RSV, which can be particularly severe in young children. But RSV is also the leadingcause ofhospitalizationsamong infants in the U.S., and in that age group, the need for new treatments remains significant.
